metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Re-audit which specific DET emitters actually fire contentless findings (without ticket IDs / file paths in evidence) — the P5 interference detector (`det_floor.py:491-509`) already emits them; clarify whether the 'subject emission fix' targets only P6 and other emitters, not P5.
findings.finding: The plan's motivating premise for COMPONENT 2 ('Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge — naming no tickets/files, unadjudicable') is refuted by the code: `det_floor.py:498-506` shows the P5 interference finding ALREADY carries `"evidence": interference[:10]`, where `_file_interference` builds entries in the form `"path: ticketA & ticketB (no ordering edge)"` — each naming the file path AND both ticket IDs. The detector already computes and emits its subjects.
findings.location: COMPONENT 2 — DET finding content floor; plan_review/det_floor.py:491-509
findings.suggested_fix: Grep det_floor.py for DetResult constructors that emit a `finding` dict with empty or absent `evidence`/`location`, and list only those as COMPONENT 2 targets. The P5 interference emitter is likely already compliant and should be excluded from the fix scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `absence_confirmed_in_context=insufficient` treatment in the veto rule (does it abstain / not trigger the veto, and if so say so explicitly as a co-located decision rule beside the `no`-trigger definition).
findings.finding: The `absence_confirmed_in_context` three-value enum (yes/no/insufficient) is introduced by the plan, but the Pass-3 veto decision rule ('claims_absence=yes AND absence_confirmed_in_context=no → dropped') never specifies the treatment of the `insufficient` third value. The plan says 'Missing answers abstain (no effect)' — but `insufficient` is an explicit answer, not a missing one. An implementer reading only the veto rule will treat `insufficient` as non-veto (the finding survives), which may be correct, but this is never stated and creates a pink-elephant antipattern: a verifier that reports it couldn't confirm or deny the claimed absence leaves the false-absence finding alive.
findings.location: COMPONENT 1 — Pass 3 veto logic / src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py (new veto rule)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line decision rule: '`insufficient` abstains (no veto); only `no` triggers the drop. A verifier that cannot resolve the lookup leaves the finding for normal Pass-3 scoring.' Co-locate this rule with the veto condition in the decide.py comment/docstring, and mirror it in the verifier prompt description.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in COMPONENT 1's Pass-3 bullet that `absence_confirmed_in_context == 'insufficient'` is treated as abstain (no veto fires), matching the symmetric behavior of the existing `cited_reference_accurate` veto and the 'missing answers abstain' rule — and add an `insufficient`-value test case to the regression fixture.
findings.finding: The Pass-3 veto rule for COMPONENT 1 specifies a three-value field (`absence_confirmed_in_context`: yes/no/insufficient) but the veto condition covers only two values explicitly (`claims_absence == yes AND absence_confirmed_in_context == no`). The plan's 'Missing answers abstain' language refers to absent/missing keys, not to the explicit `insufficient` value. The treatment of `insufficient` — whether it abstains (does not veto) or acts as a soft veto — is unspecified, creating an implementation ambiguity at a load-bearing branch in `decide.py`.
findings.location: COMPONENT 1 — Pass 3 veto description; also AC line 2 ('a regression fixture… is dropped, while a TRUE absence claim survives')
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the Pass-3 veto description: 'insufficient is treated identically to an absent answer — abstain (no veto); the veto fires ONLY on the exact pair yes/no.' Update AC2's regression fixture description to cover all three values.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicitly whether `claims_absence` and `absence_confirmed_in_context` are added to `GRADED_BINARY` (validity contributors) or to a separate non-graded veto slot in `_build_binary` (analogous to `cited_reference_accurate`), and state their default values and whether they belong in `_BINARY_NA_DEFAULT`.
findings.finding: The plan never specifies the placement of `claims_absence` and `absence_confirmed_in_context` within `verify.py`'s binary vocabulary data structures. The existing binary sub-questions are split between `GRADED_BINARY` (participate in `validity()`) and the non-graded `cited_reference_accurate` veto slot (built separately in `_build_binary`). These two new fields classify the FINDING (not the evidence), so adding them to `GRADED_BINARY` would incorrectly include them in the validity mean. The plan says only 'add two abstain-safe binaries to the shared verification contract' without specifying which vocabulary data structure (GRADED_BINARY, a new non-graded slot analogous to cited_reference_accurate, or a separate dict) they inhabit.
findings.location: COMPONENT 1 / src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify.py — `_BINARY_DESC`, `GRADED_BINARY`, `_build_binary`, `_BINARY_NA_DEFAULT`
findings.suggested_fix: Mirror the `cited_reference_accurate` pattern: add both fields outside `GRADED_BINARY` in `_build_binary` with `default='na'`, and document that `na` means the verifier did not engage the absence-claim check (abstain, no veto effect). They should NOT be in `GRADED_BINARY`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the precise drop predicate for the DET hygiene backstop (e.g., `not finding.get('location') and not any(finding.get('evidence', []))`), co-located with the backstop implementation site in `det_blocking_findings`/`det_advisory_findings`.
findings.finding: The DET-tier hygiene backstop drop predicate is under-specified: the plan says 'a DET finding with no location and no concrete evidence spans is unadjudicable and drops,' but 'no concrete evidence spans' is never defined. The existing DET emitters already populate `evidence` as a list of strings (e.g. `interference[:10]` = `['path/to/file: A & B (no ordering edge)']`; cycle evidence = `[' → '.join(cycle)]`). The backstop test author cannot write the predicate without knowing: does `evidence=[]` count as 'no concrete evidence'? Does `evidence=['']` (a list with an empty string) count? Does the predicate check `len(evidence) == 0` or `all(not e for e in evidence)`?
findings.location: COMPONENT 2 — DET backstop / src/rebar/llm/plan_review/det_floor.py:det_blocking_findings/det_advisory_findings
findings.suggested_fix: Use: `drop = not (finding.get('location') or '').strip() and not any(str(e).strip() for e in (finding.get('evidence') or []))`. Document this predicate in the plan.

findings.finding: The value vocabulary for the new `claims_absence` binary field is under-specified: the plan describes it as 'yes/no' only, while every existing binary in the contract uses 'yes|no|insufficient|na'. It is unspecified whether `claims_absence` accepts 'insufficient' or 'na', whether it is added to `_BINARY_NA_DEFAULT` (making it non-graded), and whether it lives inside `GRADED_BINARY` (affecting validity scores) or outside it like `cited_reference_accurate` (a veto-only side-channel). This placement decision is structurally load-bearing: inside GRADED_BINARY, a 'na' default changes the validity mean for every finding.
findings.location: COMPONENT 1 — absence-claim grounding veto / verify.py + _BINARY_DESC / _BINARY_NA_DEFAULT / GRADED_BINARY
findings.suggested_fix: Mirror the `cited_reference_accurate` pattern (outside GRADED_BINARY, default='na', excluded from validity). State this explicitly: '`claims_absence` and `absence_confirmed_in_context` are added to `_BINARY_DESC` and `_BINARY_NA_DEFAULT` as veto-only side-channel fields, outside `GRADED_BINARY`, with default="na" and accepted values yes|no|insufficient|na — matching the cited_reference_accurate precedent.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicitly in COMPONENT 1 that `absence_confirmed_in_context == 'insufficient'` is treated as abstain (no veto fires), matching the 'missing answers abstain' rule, so the decide.py branch author has a declared default rather than an inferred one.
findings.finding: The third possible value of absence_confirmed_in_context — 'insufficient' — is introduced in the verifier-prompt definition but its treatment by the Pass-3 veto is not explicitly stated. The veto condition fires on 'no' only, so 'insufficient' implicitly passes through as a non-veto, but this default is not declared. An executor writing the decide.py branch must infer the behavior rather than read it.
findings.location: COMPONENT 1 — Pass 3 veto description; AC line 1
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Pass-3 bullet: 'Treat insufficient identically to an absent answer — abstain (no veto).'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the COMPONENT 3 analog citation: replace `code_review/workflow_ops.py:278-301` with `code_review/workflow_ops.py:357-415` (`_det_enrich_verifications` definition + its call site in `code_review_decide`) as the actual pattern to mirror.
findings.finding: The plan's analog-citation for the COMPONENT 3 injection point ('mirroring how `code_review/workflow_ops.py:278-301` DET-enriches churn90/hard_to_reverse_surface') names incorrect line numbers: lines 278-301 contain a comment block and the `code_review_verify_inputs` function, not the DET-enrichment. The actual DET-enrichment function `_det_enrich_verifications` starts at line 357 and is called at lines 408-415 inside `code_review_decide`.
findings.location: COMPONENT 3 — operator-attested AC awareness; code_review/workflow_ops.py
findings.suggested_fix: Update the plan text: 'mirroring how `code_review/workflow_ops.py:357-415` (`_det_enrich_verifications` + its call in `code_review_decide:408-415`) DET-enriches churn90/hard_to_reverse_surface.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the vague 'sibling-overlap detector' label in the Scope with the exact file/function path where the sibling-file-interference check emits its `DetResult` (e.g. the P5 interference branch in `det_floor.py` or a specific function in `det_invariants.py`).
findings.finding: The Scope section names 'the sibling-overlap detector' as a distinct edit target, but no such file, class, or function exists anywhere in the repository under that name. The underlying check (sibling tickets touch the same files with no ordering edge) appears to be part of either P5 in `det_floor.py` or a project-DET criterion in `det_invariants.py`, neither of which is a discrete 'sibling-overlap detector' module.
findings.location: Scope section: 'plan_review/det_floor.py + the sibling-overlap detector (subject emission + the DET-tier hygiene backstop at the DET emission/aggregation point)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether `operator_attested` must be declared in `PlanSeverityAttrs` (and the output schema) or explicitly documented as a post-reshape Python-dict-only annotation that never enters the Pydantic model, to survive the forthcoming `strict=True` flip.
findings.finding: The `operator_attested` boolean field is to be injected into a finding's `severity_attributes` dict inside `plan_review_decide`, but `severity_attributes` is already shaped by `PlanSeverityAttrs` via `reshape_verifications` (which uses Pydantic's `extra='ignore'` by default). An unknown boolean field injected post-reshape into the raw dict will survive in the raw dict (it is just a Python dict at that point), BUT the plan never declares this field in `PlanSeverityAttrs` or in any schema file. If any downstream consumer validates `severity_attributes` against the Pydantic model (e.g. in a strict=True pass, a schema validator, or a test using `plan_review_decide_output.schema.json`), the field will be stripped. The plan references the injection point but never specifies that `PlanSeverityAttrs` (or the output schema) must be extended.
findings.location: COMPONENT 3 / src/rebar/llm/plan_review/workflow_ops.py:plan_review_decide / src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify.py:PlanSeverityAttrs / src/rebar/schemas/plan_review_decide_output.schema.json

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the subjective 'coaching remains non-prescriptive' clause in AC5 with a concrete observable check — for example: 'the new MOVE_REGISTRY entry contains no implementation-prescribing terms (no file names, flag names, or code constructs), as verified by grep/diff of the added entry against the existing non-prescriptive move examples.'
findings.finding: AC5 contains the clause 'coaching remains non-prescriptive' with no concrete proving command or observable mechanical check. 'Non-prescriptive' is a subjective quality judgment, not a binary observable outcome, so an agent cannot determine in-session whether this criterion is satisfied.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5
findings.suggested_fix: AC5 revised clause: 'the new coaching move's text, as diffed in the MOVE_REGISTRY, contains no file-name, flag-name, or code-construct references — verified by `grep -Ev "(\.(py|json)|def |import )" <(git diff HEAD -- src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach_moves.py | grep ^+)` returning zero matches on the added lines.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the COMPONENT 1 'Pass 1: UNCHANGED' claim to scope it narrowly to 'no new absence-detection logic in Pass 1' rather than 'no Pass 1 files touched', so it doesn't contradict COMPONENT 3's explicit rubric prompt additions.
findings.finding: COMPONENT 1 declares 'Pass 1: UNCHANGED (independence invariant, ADR 0008)' as an unconditional claim, while COMPONENT 3 explicitly modifies Pass 1 by adding ADR-0043 vocabulary to the E2/F1/E6 rubric prompts. An implementer reading COMPONENT 1 would conclude Pass 1 files are untouched; reading COMPONENT 3 they learn Pass 1 rubric prompts ARE modified. These two claims directly contradict each other about the same pass.
findings.location: COMPONENT 1 ('Pass 1: UNCHANGED') vs COMPONENT 3 ('Pass 1: the E2/F1/E6 rubric prompts gain the ADR-0043 vocabulary')
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite COMPONENT 1's Pass 1 line to: 'Pass 1: no new absence-detection logic added (independence invariant, ADR 0008); rubric prompt vocabulary additions from COMPONENT 3 are the only Pass 1 change.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan (or expand-contract sequencing) for each of the three behavioral changes: the absence-claim veto in decide.py, the DET hygiene backstop in det_floor.py, and the ac_unverifiable suppression in workflow_ops.py — e.g., a per-rule feature flag, a phased deploy with the backstop in warn-only mode first, or a named revert commit strategy.
findings.finding: The plan introduces three distinct behavioral changes to shared kernel paths (absence-claim veto in decide.py, DET hygiene backstop in det_floor.py, ac_unverifiable suppression in workflow_ops.py) but provides no explicit rollback or back-out plan if any of these changes misfire in production. The T4 criterion requires the remedy to be an explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing — acknowledgement that a change is intentional is insufficient. If, for example, the `absence_confirmed_in_context` LLM classifier begins dropping valid findings at high rate, or the DET backstop silently drops DET findings that haven't yet been updated to emit subjects, there is no described path to revert the specific rule without reverting the entire changeset.
findings.location: COMPONENT 1 (decide.py veto), COMPONENT 2 (det_floor.py backstop), COMPONENT 3 (workflow_ops.py suppression) — no rollback section anywhere in the plan
findings.suggested_fix: For the absence-claim veto and DET backstop: introduce a single env-var / config flag (e.g. REBAR_HARDENING_VETO=0) that disables only the new veto/backstop rules, leaving all other decide.py/det_floor.py behavior intact. Document in the plan that this flag is the rollback handle. For the DET backstop specifically, consider a warn-only phase (log + metric but do not drop) for one sprint before hard-dropping, to surface any emitters that were missed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR (or update an existing one such as ADR-0008) documenting the absence-claim veto: its invariant (premise-refuted drop), the abstain-safe default, the symmetry with the cited-reference veto, and the condition under which it applies — so the decision survives as a navigable reference beyond the PR.
findings.finding: The absence-claim veto is a new cross-cutting behavioral guarantee on the shared Pass-3 kernel (decide.py) — a meaningful architectural decision that changes how premise-refuted findings are handled pipeline-wide. The plan references ADR-0008 (independence invariant) and ADR-0043 (operator-attested) but proposes no ADR update or new ADR for the absence-claim veto pattern itself. Reviewers and future contributors have no durable record of why this veto exists, what invariants it maintains, and under what conditions it should be extended or removed.
findings.location: COMPONENT 1 — absence-claim grounding veto; Scope section listing decide.py and verify.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR (or targeted CLAUDE.md section) capturing the three new architectural decisions: absence-claim grounding veto, DET-tier hygiene backstop scope, and operator-attested upstream-suppression mechanism — include rationale, scope boundaries, and the regression fixture that validates each.
findings.finding: Three new architectural patterns are introduced — the absence-claim grounding veto (a new Pass-3 deterministic rule), the DET-tier hygiene backstop (a new filtering stage at det emission), and the operator-attested upstream suppression (injecting/clearing severity_attributes keys before decide.py) — but the plan's Scope and ACs mention no ADR or CLAUDE.md update to capture these decisions. 'Design approved by operator 2026-07-12' records approval but is not a durable architectural record.
findings.location: ## What (all three COMPONENTs) / ## Scope / ## Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single ADR (e.g., ADR-0044) with three sections, one per component, covering: (a) why absence-claim vetoes are applied deterministically in Pass-3 and not via threshold, (b) why the DET backstop is applied at emission rather than in pass3_over_findings, and (c) why operator-attested suppression is done upstream in workflow_ops rather than inside impact_plan/decide.py. Reference ADR-0043 and ADR-0008. Add the ADR path to the Scope list and one AC: '[ ] ADR-XXXX is merged and linked from CLAUDE.md / the review-kernel README.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a structured log line (or increment a counter metric) at each new drop/suppression point: the absence-claim veto in decide.py, the DET hygiene backstop in det_floor.py, and the ac_unverifiable clearance in plan_review_decide — include the finding index and the drop reason.
findings.finding: The plan introduces three new decision points that silently drop or suppress findings — the absence-claim veto, the DET-tier hygiene backstop, and the operator-attested ac_unverifiable clearance — but specifies no instrumentation (metric, log line, or trace event) for any of them. Operators and authors have no way to observe how often each path fires or to debug unexpected drops.
findings.location: COMPONENT 1 (Pass 3 veto) / COMPONENT 2 (DET backstop in det_floor.py) / COMPONENT 3 (ac_unverifiable cleared in plan_review_decide)
findings.suggested_fix: In each new drop path, emit a structured log entry at DEBUG/INFO level: e.g., `logger.info('finding_dropped', reason='absence_claim_refuted', finding_index=i, claims_absence=True)`. Add one AC: '[ ] Each new drop/suppression path emits a structured log entry with finding_index and reason; a test asserts the log is emitted when the path fires.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider whether C1 (absence-claim veto, the urgent lander-blocking fix), C2 (DET content floor), and C3 (operator-attested AC awareness) should be sequenced as separate child tickets, given that each is independently valuable, testable, and releasable with no order-dependency between them.
findings.finding: The plan bundles three independently valuable, testable, and releasable outcomes — (C1) the absence-claim grounding veto fixing a specific blocking false-positive, (C2) the DET content-floor hygiene fix, and (C3) the operator-attested AC suppression mechanism — each with a distinct reason to change (false-premise in Pass-3 blocking logic vs. unadjudicable DET emitter output vs. attestation-model mismatch in rubric prompts). Each component has its own AC, its own regression fixture/test, and its own file scope; none is order-dependent on the others.
findings.location: ## What / COMPONENT 1, COMPONENT 2, COMPONENT 3; ## Acceptance Criteria

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `gate_dispatch.py`'s direct calls to `det_floor.det_blocking_findings`/`det_advisory_findings` (lines 402-403 in `_degraded_plan_review_verdict`) are covered by the COMPONENT 2 backstop, and add `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py` to the scope or explicitly note it is covered by the chosen injection point.
findings.finding: `gate_dispatch.py` calls `det_floor.det_blocking_findings()` and `det_floor.det_advisory_findings()` directly (lines 402-403 in `_degraded_plan_review_verdict`) — it is a second, out-of-`plan_review/`-directory consumer of those functions that the plan does not enumerate. The plan's COMPONENT 2 backstop description names only `det_blocking_findings`/`det_advisory_findings` in `det_floor.py` and `partition_findings` in `orchestrator.py:380` as the injection points, without listing `gate_dispatch.py` in the scope. Whether the backstop covers this path depends on where exactly it is applied.
findings.location: COMPONENT 2 backstop description; Scope section listing `plan_review/det_floor.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Apply the DET hygiene backstop inside `det_blocking_findings` and `det_advisory_findings` themselves (in `det_floor.py`) rather than at a call-site wrapper — this covers all consumers, including `gate_dispatch.py`, without requiring the scope to enumerate each caller.

findings.finding: The mechanism by which the `ac_unverifiable` hard-override contribution is suppressed for an `[operator-attested]`-tagged AC finding is not specified. `impact_plan` in `decide.py` reads `attrs.get('ac_unverifiable')` directly and applies a floor of 0.85 if non-none. The plan says 'a finding whose sole basis is in-session unverifiability of a tagged criterion loses the ac_unverifiable hard-override contribution' but does not specify whether this is implemented by (a) zeroing the `ac_unverifiable` field in the injected `severity_attributes` dict before `impact_plan` runs, (b) adding a conditional branch inside `impact_plan`, (c) wrapping `impact_plan` in a new function, or (d) applying a post-compute adjustment. The decision rule and the schema that carries the flag are both undefined at the structural level.
findings.location: COMPONENT 3 — operator-attested AC awareness / Pass-3 suppression / decide.py `impact_plan`
findings.suggested_fix: A concrete approach: inject `operator_attested_ac: bool = True` into severity_attributes when the DET flag fires; in `impact_plan`, when `operator_attested_ac` is true, skip `ac_unverifiable` from the hard-override check (treat it as 'none'). Define 'sole basis' as: all other `_PLAN_HARD_OVERRIDE_AXES` besides `ac_unverifiable` are 'none' in attrs.

findings.finding: The `[operator-attested]` tag DET-detection and injection into `severity_attributes` is modelled on `code_review/workflow_ops.py:278-301`, but for plan-review the injection would need to happen in `plan_review/workflow_ops.py` (the `plan_review_decide` op, which calls `pass3_over_findings` after assembling verifications). That file is not named as an edit target, leaving the plan-review injection path unspecified.
findings.location: COMPONENT 3 — operator-attested AC awareness: 'injected into severity_attributes the way code review DET-enriches churn90/hard_to_reverse_surface (code_review/workflow_ops.py:278-301)'

findings.finding: The 'deterministic Pass-3 hygiene rule SCOPED TO DET-TIER findings' cannot be placed inside pass3_over_findings, because DET findings bypass that function entirely (they flow through det_blocking_findings/det_advisory_findings → partition_findings without entering the LLM-finding decision loop). An implementer following the plan's 'Pass-3' label will look for the hook in decide.py or pass3_over_findings, which never sees DET findings.
findings.location: COMPONENT 2 — DET finding content floor, 'Backstop' sub-paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: State the hook explicitly: 'Applied in det_advisory_findings (and det_blocking_findings for blocking DET findings) before the findings enter partition_findings — not in pass3_over_findings, which only processes LLM-tier findings.' This matches the existing DET pipeline architecture and keeps the rule co-located with the DET emitters.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
